title: Perfect Open Questions in Performance Discussion
description: User demonstrates excellent open-ended questioning about work performance
transcript_snippet:
Employee: I have been struggling with my workload lately.
User: What aspects of the workload feel most challenging for you right now?
Employee: Mainly the deadlines - they feel impossible to meet.
User: Tell me more about what makes these deadlines feel impossible.
expected_analysis: {"metrics": {"percent_open": 100, "total_questions": 2}, "code_counts": {"Q": 2, "SustainTalk": 2}, "turn_analysis": [{"codes": ["SustainTalk"], "speaker": "Employee", "rationale": "Expressing difficulty with current work demands"}, {"codes": ["Q"], "speaker": "User", "rationale": "Open question exploring specific work challenges"}, {"codes": ["SustainTalk"], "speaker": "Employee", "rationale": "Elaborating on work difficulties"}, {"codes": ["Q"], "speaker": "User", "rationale": "Another excellent open question exploring the experience"}]}
rationale: Both user questions are perfectly open-ended, using "what" and "tell me" to invite elaboration about workplace challenges rather than yes/no responses.
learning_points:
• Open questions use "what," "how," "tell me" 
• They invite elaboration about work experiences
• Avoid questions that can be answered briefly

title: Workplace Closed vs Open Question Mix
description: User mixes closed and open questions in attendance discussion
transcript_snippet:
Employee: I have been late a few times this month.
User: How many times have you been late?
Employee: About four times.
User: What has been contributing to the lateness?
Employee: Traffic and some personal issues.
User: Are you willing to work on this?
expected_analysis: {"metrics": {"percent_open": 33.3, "total_questions": 3}, "code_counts": {"Q": 1, "CQ": 2, "Neutral": 1, "ChangeTalk": 1, "SustainTalk": 1}, "turn_analysis": [{"codes": ["ChangeTalk"], "speaker": "Employee", "rationale": "Acknowledging attendance issue - shows some awareness"}, {"codes": ["CQ"], "speaker": "User", "rationale": "Closed question asking for specific number"}, {"codes": ["Neutral"], "speaker": "Employee", "rationale": "Factual response about attendance"}, {"codes": ["Q"], "speaker": "User", "rationale": "Open question exploring underlying causes"}, {"codes": ["SustainTalk"], "speaker": "Employee", "rationale": "Explaining reasons that might justify current behavior"}, {"codes": ["CQ"], "speaker": "User", "rationale": "Closed question about willingness - yes/no format"}]}
rationale: First and third questions are closed (number and yes/no), while the second is beautifully open and explores underlying causes.
learning_points:
• Closed questions limit exploration of workplace issues
• Open questions invite deeper sharing about work challenges
• Balance is important but lean toward open questions

title: Excellent Complex Reflection in Burnout Discussion
description: User demonstrates sophisticated listening with complex reflection
transcript_snippet:
Employee: I keep saying I will improve my performance, but then I get overwhelmed and fall behind again. I feel like I am letting everyone down.
User: It sounds like you are caught in a cycle that feels bigger than your conscious intentions—like part of you genuinely wants to succeed, but the workplace demands feel overwhelming, and that creates this painful gap between your aspirations and what you are able to deliver.
expected_analysis: {"code_counts": {"CR": 1, "ChangeTalk": 1, "SustainTalk": 1}, "turn_analysis": [{"codes": ["ChangeTalk", "SustainTalk"], "speaker": "Employee", "rationale": "Shows both desire to improve (change) and continued struggling (sustain)"}, {"codes": ["CR"], "speaker": "User", "rationale": "Complex reflection that goes far beyond employee words, adding insight about cycles, intentions, and systemic pressures"}]}
rationale: This reflection adds significant meaning beyond what the employee said - identifying the cycle, internal conflict, workplace pressure, and emotional impact.
learning_points:
• Complex reflections add unstated meaning about work situations
• They capture deeper emotions and workplace dynamics  
• Show sophisticated understanding of employee experience

title: Genuine Workplace Affirmation
description: User provides specific, genuine affirmation of employee efforts
transcript_snippet:
Employee: I have tried to improve my time management three times this year. Each time I start well but then slide back into old habits. I feel like I am failing at this.
User: The fact that you have made three genuine attempts shows incredible persistence and self-awareness. Many people never even try once. Each attempt taught you something valuable about what works and what does not—that is not failure, that is professional growth.
expected_analysis: {"code_counts": {"AF": 1, "CR": 1, "SustainTalk": 1}, "turn_analysis": [{"codes": ["SustainTalk"], "speaker": "Employee", "rationale": "Expresses self-criticism and discouragement about work habits"}, {"codes": ["AF", "CR"], "speaker": "User", "rationale": "Affirmation recognizes persistence and reframes attempts; also complex reflection reframing failure as growth"}]}
rationale: Excellent affirmation that specifically recognizes employee strength (persistence) and reframes the narrative from failure to professional development.
learning_points:
• Affirmations should be specific and genuine about work efforts
• Recognize effort, character, or workplace strengths
• Avoid generic praise like "good job"
• Can be combined with other MI techniques

title: Workplace Autonomy Emphasis
description: User masterfully emphasizes employee choice in performance improvement
transcript_snippet:
Employee: Everyone is telling me I need to change my approach - my supervisor, HR, even my colleagues. I feel like I am being pushed into this improvement plan.
User: I can understand how that pressure might feel overwhelming. What happens with your performance is entirely up to you—you are the only one who can decide if and when you are ready for change. No one else can make this decision for you, and frankly, no one else should.
expected_analysis: {"code_counts": {"EA": 1, "SR": 1, "SustainTalk": 1}, "turn_analysis": [{"codes": ["SustainTalk"], "speaker": "Employee", "rationale": "Expresses feeling pressured about workplace change, which often creates resistance"}, {"codes": ["EA", "SR"], "speaker": "User", "rationale": "Strongly emphasizes autonomy and choice; also reflects the feeling of pressure"}]}
rationale: Perfect autonomy emphasis that directly addresses the pressure dynamic and firmly places control with the employee.
learning_points:
• Emphasize employee choice when they feel pressured about work changes
• Use words like "your choice," "up to you," "your decision"
• Reduces resistance by removing workplace pressure
• Empowers employee self-determination

title: Workplace Persuasion Trap
description: User falls into persuasion despite good intentions in attendance discussion
transcript_snippet:
Employee: I do not think my attendance is really that bad. I only miss occasionally.
User: But even occasional absences can be problematic for the team. You have missed eight days this quarter and it is affecting project deadlines. You really need to see that this is impacting your colleagues and the department. The pattern is clear that you have an attendance problem.
expected_analysis: {"code_counts": {"C": 1, "Per": 1, "SustainTalk": 1}, "turn_analysis": [{"codes": ["SustainTalk"], "speaker": "Employee", "rationale": "Minimizing the attendance problem, defending current behavior"}, {"codes": ["Per", "C"], "speaker": "User", "rationale": "Arguing for change and confronting employee perspective"}]}
rationale: User falls into the "righting reflex" by arguing and trying to convince. This approach typically increases resistance in workplace conversations.
learning_points:
• Avoid arguing for performance change - let employee make the case
• Do not try to convince or persuade about work issues
• When employee minimizes, reflect rather than confront
• Trust the employee to see their own reasons for workplace improvement

title: Question Ratio Analysis in Performance Review
description: Example for calculating reflection-to-question ratio in workplace setting
transcript_snippet:
Employee: I want to improve but I am overwhelmed with the workload.
User: What about the workload feels most overwhelming? [Q]
Employee: I do not know how to prioritize all these competing demands.
User: That is a significant challenge - not knowing how to prioritize when everything feels urgent. [CR]
Employee: Exactly. Should I focus on quality or quantity?
User: Should you? [CQ]
Employee: I am not sure.
User: You are feeling uncertain about where to direct your energy. [SR]
rationale: Good balance of questions and reflections in workplace discussion. The 1:1 ratio is excellent for MI. Mix of open/closed questions and simple/complex reflections.
learning_points:
• Aim for reflection-to-question ratio of 1:1 or higher in workplace conversations
• Balance open and closed questions (lean toward open)
• Mix simple and complex reflections based on workplace situation
• Track ratios to monitor MI adherence in performance discussions

title: Employee Motivation Scaling in Burnout Discussion
description: Demonstrates importance and confidence scaling for workplace change
transcript_snippet:
Employee: I know I need better work-life balance, but with these deadlines...
User: On a scale of 1-10, how important is achieving better work-life balance for you right now? And separately, how confident do you feel that you could make some changes, even small ones?
Employee: Importance is probably a 9. I am burning out. Confidence? Maybe a 4. I do not see how with my current workload.
User: That is a really high importance score - a 9 - which says a lot about how much this matters to you right now. What makes it a 9 rather than a 6 or 7?
rationale: Excellent use of scaling questions for workplace change motivation, followed by good affirmation of the high importance score and exploration of what drives that motivation.
learning_points:
• Scaling questions help measure workplace change motivation
• Separate importance from confidence in workplace contexts
• High importance with low confidence suggests need to build self-efficacy
• Explore what drives high importance scores about work changes

title: Burnout and Values Exploration
description: Complex workplace conversation about values and current reality
transcript_snippet:
Employee: I used to love this job, but now I just feel empty. I go through the motions but I do not care anymore. I worry I am becoming a bad employee.
User: It sounds like there is a painful gap between the engaged, caring employee you once were—and still are deep down—and this current experience of just surviving day to day. What did loving this job look like for you before? What made it meaningful?
Employee: I felt like I was making a difference. I had energy for my team, for solving problems. Now I just want to get through each day.
expected_analysis: {"code_counts": {"Q": 1, "CR": 1, "ChangeTalk": 1, "SustainTalk": 1}, "turn_analysis": [{"codes": ["SustainTalk"], "speaker": "Employee", "rationale": "Expressing disconnection and concern about work performance"}, {"codes": ["CR", "Q"], "speaker": "User", "rationale": "Complex reflection about past vs present self, plus evocative question about values"}, {"codes": ["ChangeTalk"], "speaker": "Employee", "rationale": "Expressing what they valued about work - making a difference, having energy"}]}
rationale: Excellent complex reflection that captures the painful contrast between past and present work experience, followed by an evocative question that helps reconnect with work values.
learning_points:
• Complex reflections can highlight discrepancies between past and present work experience
• Evocative questions about past work satisfaction can reconnect employees with values
• Values exploration is powerful in workplace burnout conversations
• Focus on what made work meaningful before current struggles
